one_line_takeaway: RoSeMary pairs a CodeT5-backboned learned code watermark with a Halo2 zero-knowledge verifier, hitting 0.97 detection AUROC at near-zero functionality loss and 120 ms private verification, but only at a tiny 4-bit payload that collapses to 0.88 AUROC by 8 bits.
key_finding: On HumanEval/MBPP/EvalPlus, RoSeMary reaches 0.97 detection AUROC while keeping the watermarked code's functional pass rate essentially intact (95.12% / 97.64% / 95.39%), beating SrcMarker by an average 6.59% AUROC and 26.61% TPR, and beating inference-based SWEET whose AUROC is ~11.34% lower. It stays robust (>0.93 AUROC under variable-rename attack even at 50% variables replaced; 0.73 AUROC under refactor attack). The ZK verification is practical: a proof is generated once in 6.79 s (2.79 GB RAM, 18.75 KB proof) and each verification runs in ~120 ms (227.88 MB RAM, 511 KB verifier key).
core_contribution: RoSeMary is presented as the first ML/Crypto codesign watermarking framework for LLM-generated code. Its two novel pieces (as distinct from prior work it cites) are: (1) a learned watermark insertion/extraction pipeline that uses a pre-trained CodeT5 encoder as the insertion backbone to extract richer code features, trained end-to-end to jointly optimize the detectability-fidelity-robustness tri-objective; and (2) a secure verification layer that wraps watermark extraction in a non-interactive zero-knowledge proof (Halo2-based zk-SNARKs via a customized EZKL) so an LLM owner can prove a code snippet carries their signature WITHOUT revealing the signature, avoiding the usual need to re-encode a new watermark after each disclosure. The paper contrasts itself against inference-based watermarking (KGW, SWEET) and the prior neural approach SrcMarker (a shallow transformer trained from scratch); RoSeMary's claimed edge over SrcMarker is the CodeT5 backbone, and over inference-based methods is preserving code compilability.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 3.1. The asset is the owner's watermark signature embedded in LLM-generated code. The adversary is a malicious end user who wants to keep the code's functionality while stripping the embedded signature (e.g., to hide that code is machine-generated for plagiarism or to slip vulnerable code into repos). The adversary has general knowledge of the watermarking framework but cannot access or manipulate the owner's insertion/extraction modules. Two concrete removal attacks are modeled: Variable-rename Attack (VA), renaming variables with similar-meaning words, and Refactor Attack (RA), refactoring the watermarked code with an open-source code LLM (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct). A secondary threat addressed by the ZKP layer is signature exposure during third-party verification.
limitations: (1) Watermark capacity is very low and degrades sharply with length: AUROC drops from 0.97 at 4-bit to 0.88 at 8-bit (Table 6), and headline results use only a 4-bit payload, which limits how much owner/signature information can actually be encoded. (2) Robustness to the refactor attack is modest (0.73 AUROC) versus the near-perfect clean numbers. (3) The ZK layer proves only that extraction yields a low bit-error-rate against a signature the owner supplies at verification time; it does not by itself prevent an owner from claiming ownership of arbitrary code, and soundness of that binding is not deeply analyzed. (4) The authors' own Impact Statement flags a real-world false-positive risk: human-written code can be misclassified as LLM-generated, enabling false accusations (FPR is 0.05-0.07 in the tables). (5) The ReLU polynomial approximation and BF16 quantization introduce approximation error whose effect on detection is asserted ("virtually no loss") rather than quantified against the unquantized decoder. (6) Evaluation is Python-only and on small function-level benchmarks; SrcMarker had to be re-trained with RoSeMary's transformations since it did not natively support Python, which complicates the head-to-head. (7) Minor internal inconsistency: Table 1's caption names DS-1000 while its columns and the setup text use EvalPlus.

datasets_used: MBPP (Austin et al., 2021) - eval and all ablations/robustness
EvalPlus = HumanEval+ and MBPP+ (Liu et al., 2023) - eval (Table 1 caption erroneously says DS-1000)
CodeSearchNet (Husain et al., 2019) - pre-training corpus for RoSeMary (and re-trained baselines)
quantitative_results: RoSeMary detectability (Table 1): HumanEval Pass 95.12% / AUROC 0.97 / TPR 0.98 / FPR 0.06; MBPP Pass 97.64% / 0.97 / 0.99 / 0.05; EvalPlus Pass 95.39% / 0.97 / 0.98 / 0.06
Avg advantage over SrcMarker: +6.59% AUROC, +26.61% TPR; both keep <5% pass-rate drop

SWEET is ~11.34% lower AUROC and ~10.24% larger pass-rate drop than RoSeMary; KGW causes 48.69% avg pass-rate drop for 0.78 AUROC; REMARK-LLM gets high AUROC (0.97-0.98) but 0% pass rate (non-compilable code)
Robustness (Fig 3, MBPP): RoSeMary >0.93 AUROC under variable-rename attack at 50% variables replaced (SrcMarker/SWEET 0.07/0.21 lower); 0.73 AUROC under refactor attack
ZK overhead (Table 2): proof generation once = 6.79 s, 2.79 GB RAM, 18.75 KB proof; verification per WM = 120 ms (avg over 25 examples), 227.88 MB RAM, 511 KB verifier key (<1 MB communication)
Insertion overhead (Table 5): SWEET 0.234 s, SrcMarker 0.021 s, RoSeMary 0.027 s (~90% faster than SWEET)
Decoder ablation (Table 4, MBPP): Sd1-only 0.81 AUROC / 0.36 TPR; Sd2-only 0.73 / 0.24; both 0.97 / 0.99
Loss-weight ablation (Table 3, MBPP): (wf,wd,wr)=(0.1,0.8,0.1) best at 0.97 AUROC / 0.98 TPR; (0.8,0.1,0.1) drops to 0.95/0.93
Message-length ablation (Table 6, MBPP): 4-bit 0.97 AUROC / 0.99 TPR vs 8-bit 0.88 AUROC / 0.73 TPR
Groth16 zk-SNARK proofs are constant 128 bytes (background); RoSeMary instead uses Halo2 with larger (tens-hundreds KB) proofs for prover scalability and updatable setup
baselines_compared: KGW / KWG (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023) - inference-based NL watermark, uses Qwen2.5-Coder-14B
REMARK-LLM (Zhang et al., 2024) - neural NL watermark, re-trained with CodeT5 backbone
SWEET (Lee et al., 2024) - inference-based code watermark, uses Qwen2.5-Coder-14B
SrcMarker (Yang et al., 2024) - neural dual-channel code watermark, re-trained on CodeSearchNet with RoSeMary's Python transformations
ZKROWNN (Sheybani et al., 2023) - discussed as prior Groth16 ZK watermark verification for DNN IP, not a table baseline
